title: Riemann hypothesis
statement: Do all nontrivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) lie on the critical line Re(s) = 1/2? Equivalent to the sharpest possible error term in the prime-counting function. (Smale poses it, with the generalized RH, as his first problem; also a Clay Millennium Problem.)

unknowns.description: a proof that every nontrivial zero has real part 1/2 (or a zero off the line)

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the critical-line statement
exhibit a nontrivial zero with Re(s) ≠ 1/2

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

refs: partial: >2/5 of zeros on the line (Conrey 1989); first ~10¹³ zeros verified numerically

title: Poincaré conjecture
statement: Every simply connected closed 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere S³. RESOLVED: Grigori Perelman (2002–2003) proved it (and geometrization) via Ricci flow with surgery.

unknowns.description: a proof that a simply connected closed 3-manifold is S³

criteria.test: an accepted proof of the 3-dimensional Poincaré statement

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: P versus NP
statement: Is P = NP — can every problem whose solution is verifiable in polynomial time also be solved in polynomial time? The central open problem of computational complexity (also a Clay Millennium Problem).

unknowns.description: a proof that P = NP (an algorithm) or that P ≠ NP (a separation)

criteria.test: an accepted polynomial-time algorithm for an NP-complete problem
an accepted proof of a super-polynomial lower bound (P ≠ NP)

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: Finiteness of central configurations in the n-body problem
statement: For n point masses, is the number of central configurations (relative equilibria, counted up to rotation and scaling) finite for every choice of positive masses? Open in general; known finite for n ≤ 4 (Hampton–Moeckel) and n = 5 outside a measure-zero mass set (Albouy–Kaloshin).

unknowns.description: a proof of finiteness for all n and all positive masses (or an infinite family)

criteria.test: an accepted finiteness proof for general n
exhibit masses with infinitely many central configurations

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: Distribution of points on the 2-sphere
statement: For each N, distribute N points on the unit 2-sphere to nearly minimize the logarithmic energy −Σ log‖xᵢ−xⱼ‖. Is there a real-number algorithm, polynomial in N, producing a configuration whose energy is within an additive constant·log N of the minimum? (An extremal point-configuration / algorithmic geometry question.)

unknowns.description: a polynomial-time algorithm producing near-minimal-energy point sets on S²

criteria.test: an accepted algorithm meeting the additive-approximation target

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

refs: related to elliptic Fekete points and Thomson-type energy minimization

title: Linear programming in strongly polynomial time
statement: Is there a strongly polynomial algorithm for linear programming — deciding feasibility / optimality with a number of arithmetic operations polynomial in the number of variables and constraints only, independent of the bit-size of the data? Weakly polynomial algorithms exist (ellipsoid, interior-point); a strongly polynomial one is open.

unknowns.description: a strongly polynomial LP algorithm, or a proof none exists in the model

criteria.test: an accepted strongly polynomial algorithm for LP

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

refs: ellipsoid (Khachiyan 1979) and interior-point (Karmarkar 1984) are weakly polynomial
strongly polynomial for special cases (e.g. Tardós)

title: Hilbert's 16th problem (second part): limit cycles
statement: Is there a uniform bound H(n), depending only on the degree n, on the number of limit cycles of a planar polynomial vector field of degree n? Open even for n = 2. (Individual fields have finitely many limit cycles — Écalle, Ilyashenko — but no uniform bound is known.)

unknowns.description: a uniform finite bound H(n) on limit cycles by degree (or a proof none exists)

criteria.test: an accepted uniform bound H(n) on the number of limit cycles

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: The Lorenz attractor is a strange attractor
statement: Do the Lorenz equations, at the classical parameters, support a robust strange attractor (a singular-hyperbolic attractor with sensitive dependence)? RESOLVED: Warwick Tucker (2002) gave a rigorous computer-assisted proof that they do.

unknowns.description: a rigorous proof that the Lorenz system has a robust strange attractor

criteria.test: an accepted proof characterizing the Lorenz attractor

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: Navier–Stokes existence and smoothness
statement: For the 3-D incompressible Navier–Stokes equations with smooth, finite-energy initial data, do smooth solutions exist for all time (existence and regularity)? Or can solutions form singularities in finite time? Open (also a Clay Millennium Problem).

unknowns.description: a proof of global smooth existence, or a finite-time blow-up example

criteria.test: exhibit smooth data whose solution blows up in finite time

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.

title: Solving polynomial equations in polynomial time
statement: Can a system of n complex polynomial equations in n unknowns be solved approximately, on average, in polynomial time by a uniform algorithm (in the Blum–Shub–Smale real-computation model)? RESOLVED: Beltrán–Pardo (randomized) and Lairez (2017, deterministic) gave average-case polynomial-time algorithms.

unknowns.description: a uniform average-case polynomial-time solver for complex polynomial systems

provenance.source: Smale (2000), Mathematical Problems for the Next Century
provenance.note: Structured facts paraphrased from the historical problem list; the backlink is the attribution.
